reason:
Both correctly conclude exactly-once delivery is impossible (two generals problem), correctly distinguish delivery from processing, and converge on the same standard fix: keep at-least-once delivery and add idempotent/dedup processing, citing the outbox pattern and noting Kafka/Flink "exactly-once" is really exactly-once semantics scoped to their own boundaries, with external side effects (email, API calls) needing separate idempotency handling.

A includes two operational specifics B omits: (1) that the dedup check-and-mark-processed step must itself be atomic/in the same transaction as processing (a common subtle bug even when people think they've added dedup), and (2) that the dedup store's retention must cover the max redelivery window. These are concrete, actionable details that would change how someone implements the fix, not just restated caveats. B is more polished and adds SQS FIFO as an extra example, but that's the same kind of addition A already makes with Kafka/Flink, so it doesn't offset A's edge.

A's shorthand/lowercase style is a minor readability cost but doesn't affect correctness or usefulness. Overall A has a small substantive edge; B is cleaner but slightly thinner on implementation detail.
